name: NREM Parasomnia Residence Capacity Strategy
consequences: Immediate: The operational footprint and staffing plan are aligned with the chosen capacity to ensure safe occupancy and workflow. Systemic: Capacity expands from 8 to 12 suites, boosting nightly throughput by 50% and driving approximately 12% higher per-participant operating costs. Strategic: Accelerates enrollment and data yield for Aim 2, increasing statistical power, but increases long-term cost pressure and potential staffing bottlenecks if recruitment lags. Trade-off: This expansion accelerates data collection (speed) at the expense of higher ongoing costs and staffing risk if recruitment underperforms.
options: Maintain the 8-suite footprint and phase expansion only after the pilot confirms safety and data quality.
Scale to 12 suites with enhanced night coverage and modular staffing plans to support higher throughput.
Pivot to modular, privacy-preserving care pods deployed across a network via a data-sharing service with on-demand analytics delivered to partner sites.
review: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term regulatory changes and funding volatility.

name: NREM Parasomnia Recruitment & Consent Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Expand outreach and streamline consent to reduce pre-screening bottlenecks and maintain ethical rigor. Systemic: Enrollment rate per month increases from roughly 2 to 3.5 participants, enlarging the eligible pool and improving representativeness. Strategic: Broadens generalizability and resilience against site-specific shocks but raises oversight complexity and potential variability in screening stringency. Trade-off: Faster recruitment may dilute sample consistency and complicate protocol adherence.
options: Maintain a tightly controlled single-site recruitment with stringent pre-admission screening and comprehensive consent.
Broaden recruitment to regional sleep clinics and neurologists with streamlined consent and remote pre-screening to boost enrollment.
Launch a dynamic, remote-friendly recruitment engine with adaptive inclusion criteria and community partnerships, financed by a pay-for-performance recruitment service.
review: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant adherence and potential burnout or attrition due to remote recruitment.

name: NREM Parasomnia Data Governance & Sharing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Introduce de-identified pipelines and strict access controls with local storage and clinician-scored outcomes to minimize exposure. Systemic: Privacy incidents decline by ~40% while data processing overhead increases by ~15%, reflecting governance tightening and audit requirements. Strategic: Enables broader collaborations and secondary analyses, but imposes tighter governance and potential delays in data release and external access. Trade-off: Privacy/security versus data accessibility and speed to analysis.
options: Implement de-identified pipelines and strict access controls with local storage and offline scoring.
Adopt a multi-tier data governance model with consent management and controlled external access to support broader collaboration.
Implement privacy-preserving federated analytics across a multi-site consortium with a pay-per-use analytics service.
review: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider data governance overhead and potential misalignment with university policies.

name: NREM Parasomnia Event-Triage & Scoring Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish a baseline triage workflow with manual scoring augmented by limited automated cues to reduce reviewer burden. Systemic: Time to adjudicate events improves by ~30% with a marginal 2–3% increase in false positives, affecting precision temporarily. Strategic: Improves throughput and reduces manual workload, but introduces a risk of misclassification that could bias downstream phenotyping. Trade-off: Throughput versus accuracy.
options: Maintain full manual scoring by two independent raters and pilot limited automated cues.
Deploy a rule-based triage engine to flag probable episodes for expedited review, reducing reviewer workload.
Deploy on-device, privacy-preserving triage with edge computation and crowdsourced adjudication, offered as a pay-per-analysis service.
review: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider the reliability of edge devices and data latency.

name: NREM Parasomnia Data Quality & Dissemination Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Implement standardized calibration and enhanced training to stabilize annotation quality. Systemic: Inter-rater reliability improves from κ≈0.75 to ≈0.85, and nightly annotation throughput increases by ~20%, enabling faster turnaround for Aim 2. Strategic: Higher-quality datasets support robust phenotyping and more credible publications, but require additional upfront time and ongoing calibration costs. Trade-off: Quality versus speed/cost.
options: Maintain strict annotation training and two independent raters, with de-identified data deposition.
Invest in calibration workshops and standardized guidelines to improve inter-rater reliability and throughput.
Establish a federated, hardware-accelerated annotation lab with a pay-for-participation annotation consortium and open metadata sharing.
review: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider data access for external collaborators and risk of data leakage.

name: NREM Parasomnia Longitudinal Phenotyping Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Data harmonization across sites begins to enable standardized phenotype definitions during analyses → Systemic: Cross-site comparability improves by 20% while coordination overhead increases by 12% due to governance requirements, trading off speed of data exchange for consistency → Strategic: Enables broader multi-site validation and generalizability but increases long-term management costs and logistical complexity.
options: Maintain the current single-site phenotyping cadence with fixed nightly monitoring and centralized scoring to preserve data integrity.
Scale to regional harmonization by deploying standardized protocols and cross-site scoring guidelines to improve comparability.
Launch a privacy-preserving distributed analytics ecosystem that licenses aggregated phenotyping results to external researchers on a pay-per-use basis.
review: Controls cross-site comparability vs. coordination overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term regulatory changes and funding volatility.

name: Residential Safety & Domestic Comfort Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Participant safety and comfort during nocturnal monitoring improve due to enhanced domestic safety measures → Systemic: Incident rate declines by 40% and nightly operating costs rise by 6% due to enhanced safety protocols, trading off reduced risk against higher expenses → Strategic: Over time, improved safety and comfort boost recruitment and retention but require sustained budget allocations.
options: Preserve domestic ambience with enhanced safety features and continuous staff oversight to minimize intrusion.
Reconfigure space into clearly separated clinical zones for safety and privacy while preserving the domestic feel.
Deploy a privacy-preserving smart-safety retrofit with edge-enabled sensors and modular care pods that can be deployed off-site if needed.
review: Controls participant safety vs. budgetary discipline. Weakness: The options fail to consider recruitment attrition and regulatory oversight fatigue.

name: Sensing Infrastructure Resilience Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Core sensing setup becomes more reliable through standardized calibration and added redundancy checks → Systemic: Data completeness improves by 15% while maintenance/calibration workload increases by 12%, trading off data integrity for operational burden → Strategic: Long-term data quality supports robust phenotyping and publication while requiring ongoing calibration resources.
options: Consolidate sensing into a core calibrated set to reduce maintenance complexity while preserving critical signals.
Implement cross-site sensor calibration across collaborating sites to improve data quality and reduce drift.
Adopt a privacy-preserving distributed sensing fabric with on-device processing and a pay-per-use access model for external researchers.
review: Controls privacy vs data accessibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-site legal compliance changes and vendor lock-in.

name: Adaptive Recruitment & Retention Modeling Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Recruitment processes become more efficient via remote pre-screening and consent workflows → Systemic: Enrollment rate rises to 3.5 participants per month and screening time per participant falls by 25%, but oversight complexity increases by 15% → Strategic: Accelerates cohort assembly and generalizability but requires stronger governance and training, increasing ongoing cost.
options: Maintain a tightly controlled single-site recruitment with stringent pre-admission screening and comprehensive consent.
Pilot remote pre-screening and consent workflows with streamlined ethics approvals to boost enrollment while preserving rigor.
Launch a community-based, decentralized enrollment network with performance-based funding and dynamic inclusion criteria to maximize scale.
review: Controls recruitment speed vs. sample representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant burnout and attrition risk due to remote or high-pressure recruitment.

name: Data Transparency & Stakeholder Engagement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: De-identified datasets are prepared for controlled release, improving transparency → Systemic: Public outputs increase by 25% and external data-access requests rise by 40%, trading off governance overhead for broader engagement → Strategic: Strengthens legitimacy and opportunities for collaboration but requires robust governance and ongoing privacy safeguards.
options: Publish de-identified datasets on a controlled-access basis to support replication while preserving privacy.
Institute quarterly open briefings for participants, partners, and regulators to improve transparency and governance.
Launch a privacy-preserving, multi-site data sharing framework with paid access for external analyses.
review: Controls data openness vs privacy protections. Weakness: The options fail to consider misinterpretation risk and need for standard metadata schemas.

name: Residential Monitoring Modularity Strategy
consequences: Immediate: The footprint becomes modular to accommodate rapid reconfiguration → Systemic: Capacity grows from 8 to 12 suites, delivering a 50% increase in nightly throughput while operating costs rise by about 8% per cycle → Strategic: Accelerates data yield for longitudinal phenotyping but heightens budget exposure if recruitment slows. It preserves safety and privacy by design but adds ongoing maintenance and supply-chain risk. Trade-offs: speed of data capture versus cost discipline.
options: Maintain the current on-site footprint and central scoring to preserve reliability and avoid disruption.
Scale to modular capacity with standardized automation of routine scoring and adaptive staffing to increase throughput.
Deploy a radical on-device federated analytics network with a subscription-based researcher access model to enable privacy-preserving cross-site phenotyping.
review: Controls speed of data capture vs cost discipline. Weakness: The options fail to consider supply-chain resilience and long-term maintenance costs under extended operating conditions.

name: Cross-Institutional Phenotyping Alignment Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiates cross-site data harmonization and joint standardization of scoring → Systemic: Cross-site phenotyping consistency improves by 20% while governance overhead increases by 12% to manage policy differences → Strategic: Enables robust external validation and generalizability but raises long-term coordination costs. This requires dedicated liaisons and regular protocol updates. Trade-offs: breadth and comparability versus administrative burden.
options: Maintain strict single-site standardization with internal scoring and governance.
Implement cross-site scoring guidelines and liaison roles to improve comparability while increasing overhead.
Launch a radical interoperability platform with a subscription-based access for external researchers to enable shared phenotyping across sites.
review: Controls cross-site comparability vs administrative burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider evolving regulatory landscapes.

name: Federated Annotation & Analytics Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Annotation migrates toward distributed processing with edge-enabled workflows → Systemic: Cross-site scoring consistency improves by 15% while data latency increases by 5–7% due to synchronization → Strategic: Enables scalable validation and external collaborations but requires governance and ongoing maintenance. Latency and synchronization must be mitigated with robust tooling. Trade-off: accessibility versus governance overhead.
options: Maintain centralized annotation with local data storage to minimize system changes.
Introduce semi-automated annotation with server-based scoring and cross-site checks to boost throughput.
Deploy a radical on-device federated analytics network and a subscription-based researcher access model to enable privacy-preserving cross-site phenotyping.
review: Controls data accessibility vs governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider latency and synchronization challenges.

name: Edge-Computing Triage & Auto-Scoring Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Triage processing gains local processing to expedite flags → Systemic: Reviewer workload decreases by ~30% but false positives rise by 2–3% due to automation → Strategic: Improves throughput and reduces labor costs but risks misclassifications that could bias phenotyping. Validation and continuous monitoring are required. Trade-off: throughput versus accuracy.
options: Maintain the current triage workflow with full manual scoring to maximize accuracy.
Incorporate edge-based auto-scoring cues and lightweight triage to reduce reviewer load.
Deploy radical on-device triage with privacy-preserving technology and a pay-per-analysis model for external researchers.
review: Controls throughput vs accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider validation of edge devices in varying clinical conditions.

name: Participant-Centric Enrollment & Retention Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Consent and enrollment workflows become more participant-friendly, enabling remote pre-screening → Systemic: Enrollment rate rises by 2–3 participants per month and dropout declines by 10–15%, but oversight complexity grows by ~15% due to remote interactions → Strategic: Improves cohort representativeness and retention but demands stronger governance and privacy safeguards. Additional considerations include consent fatigue and participant burnout. Trade-off: recruitment speed versus privacy/regulatory burden.
options: Maintain current enrollment with in-hospital consent and fixed compensation.
Enhance engagement with flexible scheduling and remote pre-screening while preserving ethics.
Launch a radical consent and retention platform with mobile-native privacy-preserving data capture and dynamic compensation, supported by external research partnerships.
review: Controls recruitment speed vs privacy/regulatory burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent fatigue and participant burnout due to remote engagement.
